skill.name: routeros-fundamentals
skill.description: RouterOS v7 domain knowledge for AI agents. Use when: working with MikroTik RouterOS, writing RouterOS CLI/script commands, calling RouterOS REST API, debugging why a Linux command fails on RouterOS, or when the user mentions MikroTik, RouterOS, CHR, or /ip /system /interface paths. Scope: RouterOS 7.x (long-term and newer) only — v6 is NOT covered and accuracy for v6 problems will be low.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for working with MikroTik RouterOS v7, including CLI commands, REST API, and scripting
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Security scan detected 1898 potential issues, but all are false positives. The skill consists entirely of markdown documentation files containing RouterOS v7 reference material. The static scanner misidentified markdown code fences (backticks around code blocks) as Ruby shell execution, API documentation examples as code implementation, and common networking terminology as command-and-control indicators. No executable code, no credential exfiltration, no network access, and no malicious patterns exist. This is legitimate educational documentation for MikroTik RouterOS administrators.

content.user_title: Master MikroTik RouterOS v7 Configuration and Scripting
content.value_statement: Configuring MikroTik routers manually wastes hours on repetitive tasks. This skill gives AI agents the RouterOS v7 knowledge to write correct CLI commands, REST API calls, and automation scripts on the first attempt.
actual_capabilities: Write correct RouterOS CLI commands for interface, IP, firewall, routing, and user configuration
Construct proper REST API calls for RouterOS v7 management
Write RouterOS scripting language for automation tasks
Identify differences between RouterOS and Linux commands to prevent errors
Configure firewall rules, NAT, queues, and routing protocols
Explain RouterOS package system and licensing model
limitations: Does not cover RouterOS v6 (deprecated) - v7 commands and syntax differ significantly
Does not provide actual device access or execute commands on live routers
Does not replace RouterOS documentation for edge cases or vendor-specific features
Does not include troubleshooting beyond command syntax and API patterns

use_cases.title: Network Admin Automating Router Configurations
use_cases.description: IT administrators managing multiple MikroTik devices use this skill to generate RouterOS CLI commands and REST API scripts for bulk configuration changes across their infrastructure.

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Building Network Automation
use_cases.description: DevOps teams integrate RouterOS automation into their infrastructure-as-code pipelines using the skill to generate proper API calls and scripts for router provisioning.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers and network automation specialists

use_cases.title: Developer Troubleshooting RouterOS Errors
use_cases.description: Developers building integrations with MikroTik routers use this skill to understand why Linux commands fail on RouterOS and how to adapt their code for RouterOS v7.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers working with network infrastructure

prompt_templates.title: Basic RouterOS Command Help
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I configure [feature] on RouterOS v7? Write the CLI commands to [specific task].
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need RouterOS CLI syntax for a specific configuration task

prompt_templates.title: REST API Request Construction
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a REST API call to [endpoint] on RouterOS v7 for [operation]. Include authentication headers.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to construct API calls for RouterOS management

prompt_templates.title: RouterOS Scripting Assistance
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a RouterOS script that [description of automation task] using the scripting language syntax.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to automate RouterOS configuration with scripts

prompt_templates.title: Linux vs RouterOS Differences
prompt_templates.prompt: Why does the Linux command [command] fail on RouterOS? What is the RouterOS equivalent?

output_examples.input: How do I add a static route to 192.168.10.0/24 via gateway 10.0.0.1 on RouterOS v7?
output: Key differences from Linux: RouterOS uses dst-address instead of -net/-host, and the syntax is dst-address=<network> gateway=<gateway-ip>.

output_examples.input: Write a REST API call to list all firewall rules on RouterOS
output: Headers: Content-Type: application/json, Accept: application/json, Authorization: Bearer <token>
Authentication requires generating a token via /rest/login or using SSL certificates.

best_practices: Always specify RouterOS v7 explicitly to avoid v6 command syntax being suggested
Use the REST API with SSL certificates for production automation rather than plain HTTP
Test CLI commands in a lab environment first as RouterOS has different syntax from Linux
anti_patterns: Do not assume Linux commands work on RouterOS without checking syntax differences
Do not use RouterOS v6 documentation for v7 configuration as command structures differ
Do not skip authentication configuration when using REST API in scripts

faq.question: Does this skill work with RouterOS v6?
faq.answer: No. This skill covers RouterOS v7 only. Command syntax and API structure changed significantly from v6 to v7. Using v6 documentation with v7 routers will cause errors.

faq.question: How do I authenticate to the RouterOS REST API?
faq.answer: RouterOS v7 REST API supports token-based authentication via /rest/login endpoint with username and password, or SSL certificate authentication for production environments.

faq.question: Why do my Linux commands fail on RouterOS?
faq.answer: RouterOS is based on Linux kernel but uses a completely different CLI and command structure. RouterOS has its own proprietary shell. For example, 'ip addr show' on Linux becomes '/ip address print' on RouterOS.

faq.question: Can this skill execute commands on my router?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides documentation and command generation only. It cannot directly access or modify your router. You must implement the generated commands using RouterOS CLI or API.

faq.question: What is the difference between RouterOS CLI and REST API?
faq.answer: CLI commands are used directly on the router terminal or via SSH. REST API allows programmatic management from external systems. Both use the same underlying data model but different access methods.

faq.question: Does this skill cover RouterOS scripting language?
faq.answer: Yes. RouterOS has its own scripting language for automation. Scripts run on the router using syntax like ':put "Hello"' and can use '/path/to/command' syntax for CLI commands within scripts.
